assertion_text: High molecular weight (HMW) soluble phospho-tau isolated from human Alzheimer's disease brain tissue undergoes liquid-liquid phase separation.
A single traumatic brain injury can lead to persistent, reactive microgliosis in the white matter for up to 18 years post-injury.
LPS-induced neuroinflammation is characterized by microglia activation and neuronal cell loss in the hippocampus.
Microglial cell activation is a critical component of the neurodegenerative process in Alzheimer's disease.

Bone marrow-derived macrophages engrafted in the brain parenchyma maintain distinct transcriptomes and chromatin accessibility landscapes compared to yolk sac-derived host microglia, despite acquiring similar morphology and longevity.
Neurodegeneration is mediated by a specific set of inflammatory and neurotoxic mediators, including IL-1β, TNF-α, and ROS, released by activated microglia, astrocytes, and mast cells.
Engrafted BM-derived macrophages display discrete gene expression responses to peripheral endotoxin challenge compared to host microglia.
In the absence of microglia, amyloid-beta deposits accumulate in cortical blood vessels, resembling cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA), rather than forming parenchymal plaques.
In human HSC transplant recipients, engrafted macrophages remain molecularly distinct from host microglia, mirroring observations in mouse models.
Resident brain microglia are biologically divergent from peripherally infiltrated macrophages.
Microglial depletion reverses the altered gene expression profile observed in the hippocampus of 5xFAD mice.
Hyperphosphorylation of Tau in Alzheimer's disease leads to a loss of its microtubule-stabilizing capacity.
Microglia constitute the innate immune system of the central nervous system and serve as key cellular mediators of neuroinflammatory processes.
Mouse microglia reach transcriptional maturity by the second postnatal week.
CSF-1R is expressed exclusively in microglia and not in neurons, astrocytes, or other glial cells in the postnatal mouse brain.
Indirubin-3'-monoxime inhibits the phosphorylation of tau protein at Alzheimer's disease-specific sites in both in vitro and in vivo models.
Lithium chloride administration inhibits GSK-3 activity and reduces tau phosphorylation at Alzheimer's-associated epitopes in mutant human tau transgenic mice.
Inhibition of mTOR by rapamycin reduces Amyloid-β levels in a transgenic mouse model of Alzheimer's Disease.
Sustained microglial depletion using the CSF1R inhibitor PLX5622 prevents the development of parenchymal amyloid-beta plaques in 5xFAD mice.
Deletion of the CX3CR1 gene or anti-Aβ immunotherapy increases microglia coverage of plaques and reduces neuritic dystrophy.
Female microglia retain their neuroprotective functional ability when transferred into the brains of male mice.
Acute 7-day oral treatment with pioglitazone or ibuprofen reduces the number of activated microglia and reactive astrocytes in the hippocampus and cortex of 10-month-old APPV717I mice.
Lecanemab significantly reduces brain amyloid burden as measured by PET imaging in patients with early Alzheimer's disease.
Aβ-induced Tau missorting, microtubule depletion, and spine loss are prevented by the microtubule-stabilizing drug taxol.
Elimination of microglia rescues dendritic spine loss and prevents neuronal loss in 5xfAD mice.

Alteration of tight junction complexes and blood-brain barrier dysfunction correlate with the progression of CNS diseases including stroke, multiple sclerosis, brain tumors, Parkinson's, and Alzheimer's diseases.
Between 2000 and 2019, reported deaths from Alzheimer's disease increased by more than 145%, while deaths from stroke, heart disease, and HIV decreased.
Average per-person Medicare payments for beneficiaries age 65 and older with Alzheimer's or other dementias are approximately three times higher than for those without these conditions.
Chronic neuroinflammation and elevated cytokine levels are associated with the pathophysiology of multiple neurodegenerative disorders including MS, AD, PD, HD, ALS, and ARMD.
The number of Alzheimer's disease drug compounds progressing to regulatory review is among the lowest found in any therapeutic area.
Porphyromonas gingivalis and its toxic proteases, gingipains, are present in the brains of Alzheimer's disease patients and correlate with tau and ubiquitin pathology.
Transmembrane protein 119 (Tmem119) is a highly expressed, microglia-specific cell-surface marker in both mouse and human CNS that distinguishes microglia from other myeloid cells.
Plasma GFAP levels are significantly higher in cognitively normal older adults with high brain amyloid-β load compared to those with low brain amyloid-β load.
Core 2 biomarkers, such as tau PET and biofluid markers, provide prognostic information regarding the likelihood that Alzheimer's disease is the cause of clinical symptoms.
An abnormal result in Core 1 biomarkers (amyloid PET, approved CSF biomarkers, or accurate plasma biomarkers) is sufficient to establish a biological diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease.
Chronic microglial elimination does not alter amyloid-β levels or plaque load in 5xfAD mice.
Astrocytes, microglia/macrophages, and pericytes do not play a significant role in LPS-mediated blood-brain barrier disruption.
High-dose aducanumab did not meet primary or secondary clinical endpoints in the ENGAGE Phase 3 trial.
LPS-induced blood-brain barrier disruption is not dependent on oxidative stress.
Chronic microglial elimination via CSF1R inhibition does not alter amyloid-β levels or plaque load in 10-month-old 5xfAD mice.
The 2021 Alzheimer's disease drug development pipeline shows a diversification of target biological processes beyond traditional amyloid and tau hypotheses.
Monoclonal antibodies against Tmem119 enable the isolation of pure, nonactivated microglia from the CNS using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS).
The human BBB chip maintains high-level barrier function, mimicking in vivo conditions, for at least seven days in culture.

The inclusion of Alzheimer disease diagnoses into risk frontier calculations by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) incentivizes Medicare Advantage organizations to invest in early detection and diagnosis.
White matter hyperintensities are a core feature of Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis and represent a potential therapeutic target.

Pathological conditions such as CNS inflammation, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and epilepsy cause blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption characterized by increased paracellular permeability and leukocyte infiltration.
The success rate for advancing Alzheimer's disease drug candidates from one clinical trial phase to another is low compared to other therapeutic areas.
The volume of clinical trials undertaken for Alzheimer's disease therapeutics is disproportionately low relative to the magnitude of the public health problem.
Microglia and T cells interact in a functional equilibrium to maintain central nervous system (CNS) immunological homeostasis and influence neurodegenerative disease progression.
The 2021 Alzheimer's disease drug development pipeline exhibits a diversification of target biological processes beyond traditional amyloid and tau hypotheses.
